3: Dairy operations are becoming more industrialized and causing greater environmental impact at the same time (De Vries et al., 2012). In the U.S. most of the small farms with open animal graz- ing have already been converted to larger confined animal opera- tions (CAFOs). While manure was seen as an asset and managed without problems in smaller farms, the large volumes of manure from CAFOs is now a liability for farmers (Centner, 2006). Many dairies spread manure on fields during winter as a fertilizer (Moore and Ippolito, 2009). Land application of manure without any treatment threatens the surroundings due to high concentrations of organics, ammonia and other toxic pollutants (Shalini and

Kurian, 2012). From the manure applied to fields, nutrients, path- ogens, and other contaminants seep into the soil and reach drinking water sources threatening the public health and the environment (Williams et al., 2016). The growth rates of microalgae and algae in surface waters increase as the soluble nutrients such as nitrogen and phosphorus become available in the water (Hanifzadeh et al., 2012). Local communities are directly experiencing the eutrophi- cation impacts resulting from manure nutrients reaching surface waters (Bennett et al., 2001). For example, in Northwest Ohio 370 million pounds of manure are produced from the 37,000 dairy cows (OSU, 2006). Nitrogen and phosphorus runoffs from CAFO manures are estimated to be a large contributor to the algal blooms in Western Lake Erie Basin, of which toxins pass through drinking water treatment facilities and resulted in a drinking water ban in Toledo for three days in 2013 (Ho and Michalak, 2015). While the local runoff problems receive greater attention, manure is also a source of air emissions of methane, nitrous oxide and ammonia that cause global warming, acidification and eutrophication at

regional and global scales (Smith et al., 2009; Solomon et al., 2007). In the United States, total global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in 2014 amounted to 6,870 million metric tons (MMT) of carbon di- oxide equivalent and 9% of this amount was from agricultural emissions (USEPA, 2016).

One approach to reduction of the environmental impacts from manure runoff is to treat manure prior to land application. Treated manure can increase the nutrient uptake by crops and reduce runoff problems (Daniel et al., 1994). Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the most common method of manure treatment prior to land application (Macias-Corral et al., 2008). The main challenges for AD are long retention time and slow start-up (Poh and Chong, 2009). Moreover, AD has significant capital and operational costs and is not economically viable unless energy and fertilizer can both be sold (Franchetti, 2013).

Development of a more efficient manure treatment process is necessary to reduce the cost and environmental impacts of manure management. A rapid conversion process could possibly make holding ponds and storages unnecessary and help reduce runoffs. In this study, we address this problem by analyzing an alternative method for management of manure in which fresh manure is first dried using superheated steam drying (SSD) and then combusted as a solid biofuel. This method takes advantage of highly energy- efficient SSD technology (Fitzpatrick, 1998). The manure biosolids produced from the dryer has about 20 MJ/kg on a dry weight basis, which is similar to the energy content of low-grade coal (Liu and Tsai, 2016). So, the manure biosolids can be used to regenerate superheated steam for the drying process by heating up the spent steam from the output of dryer chamber. We evaluated the envi- ronmental performance of this technology using Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). Global warming potential (GWP), eutrophication potential (EP) and acidification potential (AP) were used as the indicators for environmental impacts. An energy evaluation of this technology (as a proxy for economic analysis) was also performed and payback time period was reported and compared to AD.

7: Two different scenarios for management of cow manure were modeled for their environmental impacts: 1) Scenario 1 is for direct application of manure on the fields (FA), and 2) Scenario 2
represents rapid drying of manure using the SSD process. In addi- tion, we compared the results from these two scenarios to literature data for AD.

The direct application of cow manure (Scenario 1) is a commonly used method for manure management as it needs low investment and the effluent is a worthy source of nutrients for crops. The first scenario is depicted in Fig. 1. The process of land application consists of two steps; storage of manure in open ponds (located within a dairy farm) for a long period of time (yearly storage was assumed in this study) and its subsequent application on land as a fertilizer.

The schematic of the SSD scenario (Scenario 2) is shown in Fig. 2a. In the SSD scenario, cow manure is stored for a short period of time (weekly storage is assumed in this study) and then trans- ferred from a dairy farm to a drying plant. The drying plant is assumed to be located at the center of northwestern Ohio region.

Fig. 2b indicates the schematic of dryer operation. During the drying process, the enthalpy of superheated steam decreases and when leaving the dryer, the steam becomes saturated steam. The saturated steam exiting the dryer may also be applied to a turbine for electricity generation. However, in our study, the used steam is recycled to the boiler to be heated to a superheated state for the next drying process. Combustion of the dried manure is the main source of heat to generate the superheated steam. The combustion residual and heat are the products of the combustion. The residual of combustion (about 15 wt% ash) was not included in our analysis.

9: The objective of this study is an environmental assessment of two different cow manure management methods for dairy farms located in northwestern Ohio. The treatment of cow manure and its conversion to a value-added product as a fertilizer or energy source was assumed as the functions of the treatment system. The func- tional unit selected for the comparison of the two scenarios was the use of 1 m³ of cow manure. The flowcharts for the direct application scenario and the SSD scenario are shown in Figs. 1 and 2, respectively. The boxes in the dashed line represent the processes that are not included in the study. Because the dryer is located away from the dairy farm while the agricultural land is usually located nearby open ponds, the transportation in the FA scenario is neglected in comparison with the SSD scenario. Construction of storage pond was also not considered as the purpose of study was to compare both sce- narios for the existing farms where the storage ponds are already constructed. Emissions from long term storage of manure and the land application of fresh manure are the parts that were considered for the first scenario. For the SSD scenario, the emissions are related to the short term storage of manure, transportation of manure from a storage tank to a dryer, construction of the dryer and operation, i.e., drying of wet manure in the SSD and combustion of the dried manure in the boiler.
The life cycle inventory data for the direct land application and

SSD scenarios are summarized in Table 1. The data for the emissions through the FA scenario is collected from Amon et al. (2006), which shows the release of various compounds to atmosphere and water during long-term storage (a year) and field application. For the SSD scenario, operation with short-term storage and weekly collection of fresh manure for transportation to the drying plant was sug- gested. In this regard, environmental emissions from short-term storage were calculated using the data source in Amon et al. (2006) and by considering a constant rate of emissions from manure during storage. The emission estimation from the trans- portation of fresh manure from a storage tank to the dryer was based on the research by Björk and Rasmuson (2002), and on the assumption that the dryer was in the middle of northwestern Ohio, which resulted in the average transportation distance of 73 km within about an hour-drive perimeter. The environmental emissions from dryer operation were assumed to occur only during the combustion of dried manure (Fig. 2b). In this regard, the emissions from operation of the dryer were calculated based on the results in Serio et al. (2002), where emission analyses were presented for pyrolysis of dried manure. The environmental emissions from dryer operation were assumed to occur only during the combustion of dried manure (Fig. 2b). In this regard, the emissions from operation of the dryer were calculated based on the results in Serio et al. (2002), where emission analyses were presented for pyrolysis of dried manure. We calculated the energy requirement for dryer operation as 352 MJ/m manure which equals to combustion of 17.6 kg of dry manure (by considering manure energy value from Table 1). Hence, the environmental impact associated with drying of 1 m³ of manure was obtained by estimating the emission originated from combustion of 17.6 kg of dried manure.

For emissions from dryer construction, Björk and Rasmuson (2002) considered steel as a main material for construction of a dryer and neglected emissions from electricity generation and addition of other substances in the construction process. Based on their study, construction of the SSD dryer with a lifetime of 15 years and drying capacity of 2.5 X 10-3 m³ requires 304 kg steel. This included the manufacturing of 10 steel pipes and two large heat exchangers which were necessary to heat up the circulating water and the boiler for superheated steam generation. As the density of dried manure is comparable to dried bark (Bjork and Rasmuson,

2002; Thirion et al., 1998), a similar amount of steel was assumed for construction of an analogous setup for drying of the fresh manure. In this regard, accounting the density of dry manure as 400 kg/m3 3 (Thirion et al., 1998) and the density of steel as 7800 kg/ m³ (Bjork and Rasmuson, 2002), 121,600 kg steel should be used to construct a dryer for the production of 1 m³ dry manure. The combustion of dry manure was assumed to be done in the boiler (i.e., superheated steam generator), and thereby the construction of the dryer included the construction of a boiler as well. Therefore, the environmental impact for construction of the dryer was calculated by estimating the total impact from the production of 121,600 kg steel.

The environmental emissions from dryer operation were assumed to occur only during the combustion of dried manure (Fig. 2b). In this regard, the emissions from operation of the dryer were calculated based on the results in Serio et al. (2002), where emission analyses were presented for pyrolysis of dried manure. We calculated the energy requirement for dryer operation as 352 MJ/m manure which equals to combustion of 17.6 kg of dry manure (by considering manure energy value from Table 1). Hence, the environmental impact associated with drying of 1 m³ of manure was obtained by estimating the emission originated from combustion of 17.6 kg of dried manure.

For emissions from dryer construction, Björk and Rasmuson (2002) considered steel as a main material for construction of a dryer and neglected emissions from electricity generation and addition of other substances in the construction process. Based on their study, construction of the SSD dryer with a lifetime of 15 years and drying capacity of 2.5 X 10-3 m³ requires 304 kg steel. This included the manufacturing of 10 steel pipes and two large heat exchangers which were necessary to heat up the circulating water and the boiler for superheated steam generation. As the density of dried manure is comparable to dried bark (Bjork and Rasmuson,

2002; Thirion et al., 1998), a similar amount of steel was assumed for construction of an analogous setup for drying of the fresh manure. In this regard, accounting the density of dry manure as 400 kg/m3 3 (Thirion et al., 1998) and the density of steel as 7800 kg/ m³ (Bjork and Rasmuson, 2002), 121,600 kg steel should be used to construct a dryer for the production of 1 m³ dry manure. Therefore, the environmental impact for construction of the dryer was calculated by estimating the total impact from the production of 121,600 kg steel.

12: 3.1. Energy payback time for the SSD scenario

The EPBT was calculated from Equation (2) as 10.5 months, 87% lower than the published EPBT (7 years) (Navaratnasamy et al., 2008) for AD. There are two factors in the SSD scenario that effectively reduced EPBT by a significant margin compared to AD: 1) Long retention time (15-30 days) for manure processing in AD method (Poh and Chong, 2009) in comparison to the much faster

SSD process (retention time < 1 h) (Fitzpatrick, 1998). This differ- ence resulted in larger volumes of the operation units for AD and consequently more required materials for their constructions. 2) High energy value of dried manure from SSD. The maximum biogas yield through AD of dairy manure is 0.16 3/kg wet-manure with a calorific value of 22 MJ/m³ (Franchetti, 2013). This can be translated to energy production of 3.52 MJ/kg wet-manure or 5.03 MJ/kg dry- manure (considering 30% wt/wt water content) through AD of dairy manure. However, dried manure from the SSD scenario has the energy value of 20 MJ/kg (Liu and Tsai, 2016), which means the produced heat from the SSD scenario is 4 times higher than the heat produced from AD.

Overall, the higher produced heat from processing of 1 m³ of manure through SSD together with lower energy input for con- struction of operational units explains the lower EPBT for SSD in contrast to AD.

The results for the eutrophication potential (EP) are summarized in Fig. 3a. Our analysis shows that 99% of eutrophication potential was related to field application where the majority of nutrient release occurred. Hence, the broadcast spreading of untreated manure is an important factor for eutrophication of water, which may cause catastrophic accidents such as algal bloom in drinking water sources. Because N and P play critical roles in aquatic eutrophication, the proper management of those components may significantly reduce the chance of algal bloom.

In the SSD scenario, a dramatic decrease in EP was observed mostly due to no nutrients leaking to resource water. The main emission which was responsible for about 72% of total EP, came from NOx and COD emissions associated with steel production for dryer construction. It was followed by eutrophication of the com- bustion process, which was approximately 23% of total eutrophi- cation. EPs for the transportation and short-term storage were about 3% and 0.3% of the total emission, respectively, and they had a minor contribution to the SSD scenario.

The results for GWP are summarized in Fig. 3b. The GWP results show that Scenario 2 (SSD) has about 70% lower total GWP than Scenario 1 (FA). In Scenario 1, manure in the open pond released a significant amount of CH4 to the air during long time storage, which accounts for the majority (99%) of GWP. In Scenario 2, the majority of GWP emissions (about 64% of total GWP) comes from the dryer construction phase (steel production). This value can vary depending on the type of dryer and the materials used in the construction phase. Therefore, a separate study is suggested to minimize the impact from the construction phase by analyzing the effects of material types and design of dryer. The storage and transportation phases take up only about 2% of the total GWP, which indicates its minor effects on GWP. The major improvement in GWP in Scenario 2 compared to Scenario 1 was resulted from the significant decrease in the manure storage time. The amount of GWP for the combustion process was estimated approximately 24% of the total GWP.

Acidification results are shown in Fig. 3c. For Scenario 1, 85% of AP was caused by NH3 during storage of manure. However, the acidification by NH3 continues also during field application, which accounts for 15% of total AP for Scenario 1. In Scenario 2, although the release of NH3 decreased during the short storage time, the reduced NH3 release from the storage didn't seem to significantly help the total AP decrease. Despite the much reduced NH3 release from storage, an insignificant improvement in AP was observed due to the productions of NOx and SO2 during dryer operation and construction phases, which are responsible for 61% and 33% of total AP, respectively. Apparently, the emissions of NOx and SO2 from the

Hence, the broadcast spreading of untreated manure is an important factor for eutrophication of water, which may cause catastrophic accidents such as algal bloom in drinking water sources. Because N and P play critical roles in aquatic eutrophication, the proper management of those components may significantly reduce the chance of algal bloom.

In the SSD scenario, a dramatic decrease in EP was observed mostly due to no nutrients leaking to resource water. The main emission which was responsible for about 72% of total EP, came from NOx and COD emissions associated with steel production for dryer construction. It was followed by eutrophication of the com- bustion process, which was approximately 23% of total eutrophi- cation. EPs for the transportation and short-term storage were about 3% and 0.3% of the total emission, respectively, and they had a minor contribution to the SSD scenario.

The results for GWP are summarized in Fig. 3b. The GWP results show that Scenario 2 (SSD) has about 70% lower total GWP than Scenario 1 (FA). In Scenario 1, manure in the open pond released a significant amount of CH4 to the air during long time storage, which accounts for the majority (99%) of GWP. In Scenario 2, the majority of GWP emissions (about 64% of total GWP) comes from the dryer construction phase (steel production). This value can vary depending on the type of dryer and the materials used in the construction phase. Therefore, a separate study is suggested to minimize the impact from the construction phase by analyzing the effects of material types and design of dryer. The storage and transportation phases take up only about 2% of the total GWP, which indicates its minor effects on GWP. The major improvement in GWP in Scenario 2 compared to Scenario 1 was resulted from the significant decrease in the manure storage time. The amount of GWP for the combustion process was estimated approximately 24% of the total GWP.

Acidification results are shown in Fig. 3c. For Scenario 1, 85% of AP was caused by NH3 during storage of manure. However, the acidification by NH3 continues also during field application, which accounts for 15% of total AP for Scenario 1. In Scenario 2, although the release of NH3 decreased during the short storage time, the reduced NH3 release from the storage didn't seem to significantly help the total AP decrease. Despite the much reduced NH3 release from storage, an insignificant improvement in AP was observed due to the productions of NOx and SO2 during dryer operation and construction phases, which are responsible for 61% and 33% of total AP, respectively. Apparently, the emissions of NOx and SO2 from the

Fig. 3. Comparison of the scenarios for (a) eutrophication potential, (b) global warming potential, and (c) acidification potential for processing 1 m³ of fresh manure through Scenario 1 (direct land application) and Scenario 2 (SSD technology).
dryer and its construction canceled out the NH3 reduction effects of short storage time. For Scenario 2, transportation also accounts for 5% of total AP.
16: 3.1. Energy payback time for the SSD scenario

The EPBT was calculated from Equation (2) as 10.5 months, 87% lower than the published EPBT (7 years) (Navaratnasamy et al., 2008) for AD. There are two factors in the SSD scenario that effectively reduced EPBT by a significant margin compared to AD: 1) Long retention time (15-30 days) for manure processing in AD method (Poh and Chong, 2009) in comparison to the much faster

SSD process (retention time < 1 h) (Fitzpatrick, 1998). This differ- ence resulted in larger volumes of the operation units for AD and consequently more required materials for their constructions. 2) High energy value of dried manure from SSD. The maximum biogas yield through AD of dairy manure is 0.16 3/kg wet-manure with a calorific value of 22 MJ/m³ (Franchetti, 2013). This can be translated to energy production of 3.52 MJ/kg wet-manure or 5.03 MJ/kg dry- manure (considering 30% wt/wt water content) through AD of dairy manure. However, dried manure from the SSD scenario has the energy value of 20 MJ/kg (Liu and Tsai, 2016), which means the produced heat from the SSD scenario is 4 times higher than the heat produced from AD.

Overall, the higher produced heat from processing of 1 m³ of manure through SSD together with lower energy input for con- struction of operational units explains the lower EPBT for SSD in contrast to AD.

18: We compared Scenarios 1 and 2 to published data for AD. The AP and GWP results for AD were obtained from Whiting and Azapagic (2014). The EP data for AD were obtained from Billen et al. (2015). All the data were normalized based on the functional unit of the present work (1 m³ of fresh manure). Fig. 4 shows the comparison between the first, second and AD scenarios. The impact values are normalized based on the scenario with a maximum impact. The comparison between scenarios indicated that the SSD process (Scenario 2) has lower GWP and EP than both Scenario 1 and AD. However, AP is higher in the SSD scenario because it produces high amounts of acid gases such as NOx and SOx in the combustion process. These combustion byproducts can be captured by a scrubber using aqua ammonia that is produced during the ammonia stripping of residual steam (not included in this study) (Resnik et al., 2004). AP of Scenario 1 (direct field application) is considerably high due to the substantial emission of ammonia

Fig. 4. Comparison of the normalized impacts for Scenario 1 (direct field application) and Scenario 2 (SSD technology) with AD. For the each impact category, the impacts are normalized with respect to the scenario that has a highest impact.

during the land application process. It is noted that the reduction in GWP for the SSD scenario is possible as a result of low CH4 pro- duction. Also, the total EP for Scenario 2 is drastically reduced compared to Scenario 1 and AD. Consequently, application of the SSD scenario to manure management can possibly be effective for preventing the local algae bloom predicament.

20: <header>3.3.2. Impacts normalized and summed with respect to person equivalent impacts in the United States </header>

Because the each impact category has different units, the values from them cannot be directly summed to estimate the 'total' environmental impact from the each scenario. This issue affects interpretation of the SSD scenario since its environmental impacts are observed to be lower in GWP and EP but higher in AP. To resolve this issue, we normalized the each impact in terms of a person's impact in the United States (Ryberg et al., 2014) and then summed these normalized impacts to estimate a people-equivalent impact score for the each scenario.

Fig. 5 shows that the SSD scenario has the lowest person impact scores of all for management of dairy manure with regards to the environmental impacts. The SSD process shows the total normal- ized impact of 8.7 people-equivalent where AD shows 28.7 people- equivalent. The eutrophication potential (EP) in Scenario 1 is comparable to the total impact produced from 186.6 people which is 21.5 and 6.5 times higher than the normalized impacts from SSD and AD methods, respectively. The high EP observed in Scenario 1 is thought to be the main drawback of direct field application. Although EP is reduced for AD compared to Scenario 1, it is still 2 times greater than that of the SSD scenario. The normalized value for GWP is 3.4 people-equivalent for Scenario 1 followed by 1.45 people-equivalent for AD and 0.9 for Scenario 2. The normalized AP results are 2.9, 1.9 and 1.15 people-equivalent for the SSD process, FA and AD, respectively. It can be readily indicated from Fig. 5 that EP accounts for the majority of the normalized impact equivalents for all three scenarios. This means that these processes mostly have eutrophication impacts on the surrounding environment. There- fore, the greater improvement in EP observed in the SSD scenario results in more environmentally efficient process overall. It should be noted that the emission for Scenario 1 (Direct application) is specified for the farms located in northwestern Ohio. Many countries have certain regulations regarding application of
Fig. 5. The impact calculation based on the number of people impact equivalent.

manure for farm fertilization. Moreover, for both FA and AD, reg- ulations are applied in some areas for covering manure storage to reduce the methane release to the atmosphere. All these regula- tions may influence the overall impact calculations of this study if a similar comparative LCA was performed for these countries and areas.

23: From this LCA analysis, we found that four further studies may be helpful to improve the SSD process: 1) A detailed economic analysis for the process design should be performed including all the necessary units such as heat exchangers and scrubbers. 2) The rapid drying process of SSD technology indicates the possibility of complete removal of storage units. Because the significant de- creases in GWP (70% lower than FA and 38% lower than AD in people-equivalent impact) and EP (95% lower than FA and 80% lower than AD) in SSD is mostly due to the short manure storage time, the future study may address how the removal or reduction of manure storage in SSD would lead to further improvement in the overall environmental impact. 3) Scrubbing units for removal of ammonia, NOx, and SO2 seem to be necessary for the SSD process because the large amount of ammonia produced during manure drying may damage the dryer. More in-depth LCA analysis for the SSD process including these units and management of the byproducts from these units would provide more accurate envi- ronmental impact data. 4) It is considered that the dried manure as the product of SSD may be used as sterilized fertilizer. Because production of N-fertilizers is energy demanding, a comparison between the proposed SSD with the one that uses the dried manure as N-fertilizer may be interesting.
24: In this study we analyzed and compared the SSD scenario (Scenario 1) to two conventional manure management methods, FA (Scenario 2) and AD, with respect to environmental emissions and energy payback. A considerably less EPBT period for the SSD sce- nario was resulted in contrast to AD, which validates the possibility for high production rates of value-added products that makes it a promising alternative for dairy manure management.

Assessments of environmental impacts for the FA and SSD sce- narios using LCA methodology demonstrated significant improve- ment in overall normalized impact values for the SSD scenario. The results of this study indicate that the SSD method can be applied to local dairy farms to reduce eutrophication and to reduce green- house gas emissions from manure management. Although AP turned out to be higher for SSD than AD, the overall normalized impact evaluation confirmed that the SSD process could be an

environmentally effective manure management method. Per our assumptions, these conclusions were made for dairy farms with storage ponds. Inclusion of storage pond construction in the anal- ysis would further increase the emissions in the scenario 1 (FA) compared to the SSD process.
